name: Capability recontextualizes old space
designProblem: Progression should deepen the meaning of known places instead of only appending new rooms.
principle: Teach a reusable capability, preserve visible unresolved affordances, then make returning a self-motivated act of reinterpretation.
counterLimit: Avoid artificial locks when the intended experience depends on broad early traversal freedom.

name: Knowledge-gated revisit
designProblem: A return should become possible through understanding rather than only through inventory acquisition.
principle: Place stable but initially unreadable evidence in an early visit, then let later knowledge reveal an action that was always physically available.
counterLimit: Do not hide required progression behind culturally specific or untelegraphed wording.

name: State axis rewrites routes
designProblem: A small map needs meaningful transformation without becoming an unrelated set of replacement scenes.
principle: Expose one legible state axis whose consistent environmental consequences open some routes while closing or changing others.
counterLimit: Do not force a binary layer swap when traversal value comes from continuous open-space navigation.

name: Parallel-state comparison
designProblem: Players need to infer a hidden difference between two versions of the same place or relationship.
principle: Keep landmarks and causal anchors stable across contrasting states so comparison itself becomes the information-gathering verb.
counterLimit: Avoid parallel states when fast cooperative readability is more important than reflective comparison.

name: Finite schedule loop
designProblem: A dense cast and small area need evolving situations while the player retains freedom to investigate.
principle: Repeat a finite, readable schedule while allowing knowledge, selected resources, and deliberate interventions to persist across resets.
counterLimit: Avoid reset pressure when the story requires uninterrupted emotional continuity or cannot explain repetition.

name: Landmark promises future use
designProblem: Exploration needs directional pull without reducing discovery to a list of explicit objectives.
principle: Show a distinctive, navigable landmark before its full meaning is known and preserve sightline or memory cues until the player can act on it.
counterLimit: Do not create distant promises in short stages whose cooperative cadence depends on immediate shared goals.

name: Loop and shortcut memory
designProblem: Repeated travel should confirm learning and progress instead of feeling like redundant backtracking.
principle: Fold routes back onto memorable anchors, then let player action open shortcuts that visibly compress a previously understood journey.
counterLimit: Do not over-compress a route whose repeated traversal is the deliberate navigation challenge.

name: Hub-spoke return cadence
designProblem: A broad adventure needs rest, synthesis, and social feedback between outward expeditions.
principle: Alternate outward challenge spokes with meaningful hub returns that change services, relationships, information, or route choice.
counterLimit: Avoid static hub resets when evolving schedules are the central dramatic system.

name: Bounded challenge as macro state machine
designProblem: A closed challenge needs global coherence beyond a sequence of unrelated rooms.
principle: Give the whole bounded space one inspectable state variable whose manipulation changes routes, clues, and local challenges across multiple zones.
counterLimit: Do not impose one global machine on a space whose intended appeal is independent route discovery.

name: Safe rule experiment
designProblem: Players need to discover a rule through action without an instruction dump or punishing first failure.
principle: Present one salient affordance in a low-risk pocket, make the first causal result unmistakable, and leave room for voluntary repetition.
counterLimit: Do not treat lethal ambiguity as teaching when feedback does not explain the failed hypothesis.

name: Teach-practice-twist-exam rule arc
designProblem: A mechanic should grow into a meaningful language instead of appearing as a one-off interaction.
principle: Teach one rule safely, require deliberate practice, vary context, combine or reverse the rule, then stage a readable synthesis exam.
counterLimit: Short cooperative stages may need a compressed arc and shared teaching rather than a long solo sequence.

name: Environmental chain reaction
designProblem: Systemic freedom needs predictable consequences rather than arbitrary object-specific scripting.
principle: Define a small set of readable material or state relations whose consequences propagate through nearby objects and remain inspectable.
counterLimit: Do not promise simulation depth when the runtime can only support isolated binary interactions.

name: Multi-solution state graph
designProblem: A problem should reward understanding and improvisation without becoming impossible to validate or recover.
principle: Define the required state outcome independently of one action sequence, then admit several verb-and-object paths that satisfy the same invariants.
counterLimit: Do not erase the value of carefully sequenced information puzzles by accepting arbitrary state changes.

name: Object-role composition
designProblem: Portable objects need to participate in a shared rule language instead of acting as isolated keys.
principle: Give objects a small set of legible functional roles and let spatial placement or combination produce consequences from those roles.
counterLimit: Avoid complex object composition when synchronized group readability is the primary constraint.

name: Copy and redeploy affordance
designProblem: The player needs a broad expressive vocabulary without a separate bespoke item for every obstacle.
principle: Let observation capture a bounded functional affordance, then redeploy that role under clear cost, placement, and coexistence limits.
counterLimit: Do not replace a deliberately authored small tool set with an unreadable catalogue of copied objects.

name: Perspective shift reframes space
designProblem: A familiar area needs new navigational meaning without discarding its landmarks or topology.
principle: Change the player’s scale, layer, plane, or embodied viewpoint while preserving anchors that make old and new spatial readings comparable.
counterLimit: Do not introduce free perspective shifts when a fixed route viewpoint is essential to control clarity.

name: Constrained vehicle route with local agency
designProblem: Travel on a vehicle or fixed network risks becoming passive traversal with little player authorship.
principle: Constrain the macro route but embed meaningful switches, hazards, timing, detours, and local disembarkation choices along it.
counterLimit: Avoid route rails when unconstrained direction choice is the central exploration promise.

name: Player-authored map memory
designProblem: The game needs to support planning and recall without automatically solving every navigation question.
principle: Let players place bounded notes or route marks that externalize their hypotheses while leaving interpretation and action to them.
counterLimit: Do not pause shared real-time play for detailed private annotation unless all players remain informed.

name: Reward expands future possibility
designProblem: Optional exploration rewards should matter beyond completion percentage or immediate currency.
principle: Reward discovery with a capability, knowledge, relationship, or resource pattern that creates new choices elsewhere in the world.
counterLimit: Keep stage rewards immediately legible when long-horizon world revisits do not exist.

name: Optional secret readability
designProblem: Secrets should reward attention and inference without requiring random exhaustive interaction.
principle: Repeat subtle visual, spatial, behavioral, or audio grammar so an observant player can form and test a secret-finding hypothesis.
counterLimit: Do not make mandatory progression depend on weak or inconsistent secret grammar.

name: Resource expedition and return
designProblem: Resource pressure should shape route planning without turning exploration into irreversible attrition.
principle: Let the player choose expedition depth against readable supplies, then convert a voluntary return into preparation and a changed next attempt.
counterLimit: Do not stack opaque resource pressure on top of a schedule loop whose reset already carries the dramatic cost.

name: Boss as rule synthesis
designProblem: A climactic encounter should test learned understanding rather than reveal an unrelated hidden trick.
principle: Recombine previously practiced signals, timing, verbs, and environmental rules under pressure while keeping failure causes readable.
counterLimit: Do not collapse an expressive multi-solution system into one prescribed boss weakness sequence.

name: Threat as navigation teacher
designProblem: Enemies should teach space, timing, and affordances rather than merely interrupt exploration.
principle: Place readable threats so evasion or engagement demonstrates route shape, safe zones, useful objects, and later mastery opportunities.
counterLimit: Avoid precision threat placement when stylus navigation or vehicle control makes the taught response unreliable.

name: NPC schedule as information
designProblem: NPC routines need to be playable evidence rather than decorative background motion.
principle: Give a character a legible routine whose deviations reveal needs, relationships, risks, or hidden causal rules the player can act upon.
counterLimit: Do not require private schedule tracking in a short synchronous cooperative stage.

name: NPC reacts to world state
designProblem: A changed map feels hollow when characters continue to behave as if nothing happened.
principle: Bind NPC availability, tactic, dialogue intent, or service state to the same causal facts that drive visible environmental change.
counterLimit: Do not imply deep social simulation when the product has almost no recurring characters.

name: Companion as contextual verb
designProblem: A companion risks becoming constant exposition unless their presence changes what the player can actually do.
principle: Express the companion through a bounded contextual action, information capability, or traversal role that evolves with the relationship.
counterLimit: Avoid a compulsory speaking companion when solitude and self-directed observation are the dominant fantasy.

name: Embodied world-change reveal
designProblem: A major story reveal should be felt as a change in action and place rather than delivered only as text.
principle: Synchronize a canon-safe reveal with visible world-state change, altered routes, actor tactics, and a new interaction window.
counterLimit: Do not interrupt a fast shared stage with a long noninteractive transformation sequence.

name: Threshold performance cue
designProblem: Entering a changed place or major challenge needs clarity without removing control for an extended scene.
principle: At a spatial threshold, briefly align framing, sound, actor blocking, and objective salience, then return agency with the next action readable.
counterLimit: Keep cues minimal when uninterrupted player-led exploration is the stronger presentation language.

name: Agency-performance breathing
designProblem: Continuous dialogue or continuous interaction both flatten dramatic rhythm and reduce comprehension.
principle: Alternate short authored performances with purposeful interaction windows whose actions prepare, complicate, or pay off the next beat.
counterLimit: Do not over-author pauses when the central promise is sustained free-form exploration.

name: Action-first onboarding
designProblem: A player who knows nothing about their role needs immediate orientation without an exposition wall.
principle: Begin with a safe, character-defining action whose feedback reveals role, local objective, and one world rule before optional context expands.
counterLimit: Do not assume players infer identity from action when essential stakes remain invisible and unsupported.

name: Reversible reset with knowledge retention
designProblem: Resetting world state risks invalidating effort unless each attempt produces durable player or system knowledge.
principle: Reset reversible world state to a clear checkpoint while retaining discoveries, selected commitments, and a faster route to the next hypothesis.
counterLimit: Avoid diegetic repetition when a one-directional emotional arc would be weakened by resets.

name: Final exam recombines familiar rules
designProblem: A finale should prove accumulated understanding instead of introducing the game’s deepest rule too late.
principle: Recombine previously operated mechanics, spatial memories, relationships, and reveal-safe facts in a new but inferable configuration.
counterLimit: Do not prescribe a single recombination when the established system promises multiple valid solutions.

name: Small systemic verb toolkit
designProblem: A game needs systemic depth without overwhelming the player with unrelated one-use actions.
principle: Choose a small verb set with orthogonal effects, let each verb solve several problem classes, and create depth through combinations.
counterLimit: Compress the toolkit when simultaneous players need immediate shared readability.

name: Dense overworld as challenge space
designProblem: An overworld should contain authored rule development rather than serve only as travel between isolated challenges.
principle: Layer landmarks, traversal tests, local state machines, shortcuts, and optional discoveries so the connecting world teaches and examines core verbs.
counterLimit: Do not simulate dense free exploration on a product whose travel fantasy depends on a clearly constrained network.

name: Cooperative role interdependence
designProblem: Cooperative play needs complementary responsibilities rather than several players performing the same action in parallel.
principle: Create simultaneous roles with shared state, visible dependencies, recovery from mistakes, and opportunities for both cooperation and playful competition.
counterLimit: Keep disabled when the runtime or story has only one player role and no robust synchronization model.

name: Asymmetric screen information
designProblem: Multiple players can hold different private information while acting in one shared world.
principle: Separate private and shared views only when communication itself is a supported verb and no player becomes a passive information terminal.
counterLimit: Research-only until a second distinct work and a supported multi-screen runtime provide evidence and validation.
